content:
Writing about what you learn is one of the best ways to solidify your knowledge. Moving from graphical interfaces to the Linux command line is a major milestone, and a tutorial makes for a perfect first Medium article.
Here is a complete, well-structured draft you can copy, tweak, and publish. It assumes an Ubuntu/Debian environment, which is standard for most beginners.
Ditching the GUI: How to Install WordPress Entirely from the Linux Command Line
When you first start learning Linux, the terminal can feel like a dark, intimidating room. But once you learn where the light switches are, you realize it’s actually the fastest and most efficient way to get things done.
Recently, I challenged myself to step away from control panels and graphical installers to do something fundamental: install WordPress using nothing but command-line instructions.
If you want to understand what actually happens behind the scenes when you click “One-Click Install” on your hosting provider, this guide is for you. We are going to build a LAMP (Linux, Apache, MySQL, PHP) stack and deploy WordPress entirely via the terminal.
Prerequisites
Before we begin, you will need:

• A server running Ubuntu (or a local virtual machine).

• Access to a terminal with sudo privileges.
Step 1: Prepare the Server and Install Apache
First, we need to make sure our package lists are up to date. Then, we’ll install Apache, which will act as our web server.
Bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y
sudo apt install apache2 -yOnce installed, we want to ensure Apache is running and set to start automatically if the server reboots:
Bash
sudo systemctl enable apache2
sudo systemctl start apache2Step 2: Install and Secure MySQL
WordPress needs a database to store all its content, user data, and settings. We’ll install MySQL for this job.
Bash
sudo apt install mysql-server -yAfter installation, it’s best practice to run the security script to lock down your database:
Bash
sudo mysql_secure_installation(Follow the on-screen prompts to set your password policy and remove anonymous users.)
Step 3: Install PHP
Apache serves the pages, and MySQL stores the data, but WordPress is written in PHP. We need to install PHP and the specific extensions WordPress relies on to connect to the database.
Bash
sudo apt install php libapache2-mod-php php-mysql php-curl php-gd php-xml php-mbstring -yRestart Apache so it recognizes the new PHP modules:
Bash
sudo systemctl restart apache2Step 4: Create the WordPress Database
Now we need to speak directly to MySQL to create a home for our WordPress data. Log into the MySQL prompt:
Bash
sudo mysql -u root -pOnce inside the mysql> prompt, run these commands (replace your_password with a strong password):
SQL
CREATE DATABASE wordpress_db;
CREATE USER 'wp_user'@'localhost' IDENTIFIED BY 'your_password';
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON wordpress_db.* TO 'wp_user'@'localhost';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
EXIT;Step 5: Download and Extract WordPress
With our server environment fully prepped, it’s time to grab WordPress. We’ll navigate to a temporary directory, download the latest version using wget, and extract it.
Bash
cd /tmp
wget https://wordpress.org/latest.tar.gz
tar -xzvf latest.tar.gzNext, move the extracted files to Apache’s root web directory so the internet can see them:
Bash
sudo mv wordpress/* /var/www/html/Step 6: Set the Correct Permissions
If the permissions are wrong, WordPress won’t be able to update plugins or upload images. We need to give ownership of the web directory to the Apache user (www-data).
Bash
sudo chown -R www-data:www-data /var/www/html/
sudo chmod -R 755 /var/www/html/(Optional cleanup: Remove the default Apache index page so it doesn’t conflict with WordPress)
Bash
sudo rm /var/www/html/index.htmlStep 7: The Final Configuration
Now, open your web browser and enter your server’s IP address.
Instead of a command prompt, you will be greeted by the famous WordPress setup screen. Enter the database name (wordpress_db), username (wp_user), and the password you created in Step 4.
Conclusion
Setting up WordPress via the command line takes a few more minutes than clicking an auto-installer, but the payoff is massive. You aren’t just getting a blog — you are getting a fundamental understanding of how web servers, databases, and permissions interact in a Linux environment.
The terminal is no longer a dark room. You just wired the electricity yourself.
